clusters.title: Caffeine reversed memory problems caused by sleep deprivation
clusters.summary: Scientists discovered that sleep deprivation damages a key brain circuit responsible for social memory, making it harder to recognize familiar individuals. In laboratory studies, caffeine restored communication between neurons in this pathway and reversed the memory deficits caused by lost sleep. The effect was remarkably targeted, helping the impaired circuit recover without overstimulating normal brain function.

items.title: Caffeine reversed memory problems caused by sleep deprivation

The effect was remarkably targeted, helping the impaired circuit recover without overstimulating normal brain function.

clusters.title: Can fasting fight gum disease? Scientists find surprising link
clusters.summary: A low-calorie fasting-style diet significantly reduced inflammation linked to gum disease in a small clinical study. The findings suggest that what people eat may influence gum health almost as much as what they do with a toothbrush.

items.title: Can fasting fight gum disease? Scientists find surprising link
items.description: A low-calorie fasting-style diet significantly reduced inflammation linked to gum disease in a small clinical study. The findings suggest that what people eat may influence gum health almost as much as what they do with a toothbrush.

clusters.title: Shockwaves from routine military duties associated with long-term anger and violence
clusters.summary: Veterans who worked in high-blast occupations, such as artillery or weapons training, show higher rates of anger and aggression. Using artificial intelligence to scan medical records, researchers found this occupational exposure carries a distinct behavioral risk.

items.title: Shockwaves from routine military duties associated with long-term anger and violence
items.description: Veterans who worked in high-blast occupations, such as artillery or weapons training, show higher rates of anger and aggression. Using artificial intelligence to scan medical records, researchers found this occupational exposure carries a distinct behavioral risk.

clusters.title: New GLP-1 diabetes pill delivers major weight loss and blood sugar control
clusters.summary: A new oral GLP-1 medication helped people with type 2 diabetes dramatically improve blood sugar control and lose weight in a major clinical trial. The results suggest that highly effective diabetes treatments may soon become available in a much more convenient pill form.

items.title: New GLP-1 diabetes pill delivers major weight loss and blood sugar control
items.description: A new oral GLP-1 medication helped people with type 2 diabetes dramatically improve blood sugar control and lose weight in a major clinical trial. The results suggest that highly effective diabetes treatments may soon become available in a much more convenient pill form.

clusters.title: New study reveals how infants’ brains and bodies respond to music in the first year of life
clusters.summary: New research reveals that while infants' brains process musical structure as early as three months old, their bodies take much longer to catch up. Structured physical movement to music doesn't emerge until closer to their first birthday.

items.title: New study reveals how infants’ brains and bodies respond to music in the first year of life
items.description: New research reveals that while infants' brains process musical structure as early as three months old, their bodies take much longer to catch up. Structured physical movement to music doesn't emerge until closer to their first birthday.

clusters.title: The deadly tapeworm spreading across America has reached the Pacific Northwest
clusters.summary: A potentially dangerous tapeworm linked to severe, cancer-like disease has now been found in the Pacific Northwest, marking its first detection in wild animals along the U.S. West Coast. Researchers discovered the parasite, Echinococcus multilocularis, in 37% of coyotes tested around Puget Sound—a surprisingly high rate for a region where it had never been reported until recently.

items.title: The deadly tapeworm spreading across America has reached the Pacific Northwest

clusters.title: Childhood hyperactivity symptoms show long-term associations with lower life quality
clusters.summary: Children experiencing high levels of inattention and hyperactivity report persistently lower social, emotional, and physical well-being throughout their developmental years. A 13-year observational study highlights the need to treat the holistic educational and psychological needs of affected youth.

items.title: Childhood hyperactivity symptoms show long-term associations with lower life quality

clusters.title: Common pesticide linked to hidden brain damage, scientists warn
clusters.summary: Scientists have uncovered alarming new evidence that a common insecticide may leave lasting marks on the developing brain before a child is even born. Researchers studying New York City children found that prenatal exposure to chlorpyrifos — a pesticide once widely used indoors and still used in agriculture — was linked to widespread brain abnormalities and weaker motor skills years later.

items.title: Common pesticide linked to hidden brain damage, scientists warn
items.description: Scientists have uncovered alarming new evidence that a common insecticide may leave lasting marks on the developing brain before a child is even born. Researchers studying New York City children found that prenatal exposure to chlorpyrifos — a pesticide once widely used indoors and still used in agriculture — was linked to widespread brain abnormalities and weaker motor skills years later.

clusters.title: A common vitamin could help fight one of the deadliest brain cancers
clusters.summary: A clinical trial is exploring whether high doses of vitamin B3 could give patients with glioblastoma a better chance against the aggressive brain cancer. Scientists found that niacin may help revive immune cells that tumors shut down, allowing them to attack cancer more effectively. Early results have been promising, with patients showing significantly better progression-free survival than expected.

items.title: A common vitamin could help fight one of the deadliest brain cancers

clusters.title: Common plastic chemical linked to lifelong anxiety in new study
clusters.summary: Exposure to a common plastic chemical before and shortly after birth may have lasting effects on behavior. Researchers found that male rats exposed early in life to DEHP—a plasticizer used in products ranging from medical devices to toys—showed significantly higher anxiety as adults, even long after exposure had ended. The animals were more hesitant to explore open spaces and spent more time frozen in place, classic signs of anxiety in rodents.

items.title: Common plastic chemical linked to lifelong anxiety in new study
items.description: Exposure to a common plastic chemical before and shortly after birth may have lasting effects on behavior. Researchers found that male rats exposed early in life to DEHP—a plasticizer used in products ranging from medical devices to toys—showed significantly higher anxiety as adults, even long after exposure had ended. The animals were more hesitant to explore open spaces and spent more time frozen in place, classic signs of anxiety in rodents.

clusters.title: Scientists found a new Alzheimer’s trigger and a drug that stops it
clusters.summary: Researchers have identified a new Alzheimer’s target and created an experimental compound that blocks a damaging process inside brain cells. In mice, the treatment slowed nerve cell loss, reduced Alzheimer’s-related changes, and even appeared to promote healthier aging.

items.title: Scientists found a new Alzheimer’s trigger and a drug that stops it
items.description: Researchers have identified a new Alzheimer’s target and created an experimental compound that blocks a damaging process inside brain cells. In mice, the treatment slowed nerve cell loss, reduced Alzheimer’s-related changes, and even appeared to promote healthier aging.

clusters.title: Scientists found a surprising problem with sugar-free diets
clusters.summary: A surprising new study suggests that completely eliminating sugar may backfire. Mice on a sucrose-free low-fat diet showed worse blood sugar control, increased inflammation, disrupted gut bacteria, and signs of fatty liver compared with mice that consumed some sucrose. Researchers say the results highlight the importance of a balanced diet and a healthy gut microbiome rather than focusing solely on cutting out sugar.

items.title: Scientists found a surprising problem with sugar-free diets
items.description: A surprising new study suggests that completely eliminating sugar may backfire. Mice on a sucrose-free low-fat diet showed worse blood sugar control, increased inflammation, disrupted gut bacteria, and signs of fatty liver compared with mice that consumed some sucrose. Researchers say the results highlight the importance of a balanced diet and a healthy gut microbiome rather than focusing solely on cutting out sugar.

clusters.title: Scientists discover the brain chemical that helps you break bad habits
clusters.summary: Scientists have uncovered a key brain signal that helps us break old habits and adapt when circumstances suddenly change. By watching mice navigate a virtual maze, researchers found that disappointment—when an expected reward failed to appear—triggered a surge of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine, making the animals more likely to try a new strategy. When acetylcholine was blocked, the mice became less flexible and were more likely to stick with outdated choices.

items.title: Scientists discover the brain chemical that helps you break bad habits

By watching mice navigate a virtual maze, researchers found that disappointment—when an expected reward failed to appear—triggered a surge of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine, making the animals more likely to try a new strategy. When acetylcholine was blocked, the mice became less flexible and were more likely to stick with outdated choices.

clusters.title: Your brain was never designed for this much bad news
clusters.summary: Humans evolved to pay close attention to danger, but today that instinct is being overwhelmed by an endless supply of bad news from around the world. Researchers say the answer isn’t to stop following current events—it’s to build healthier habits around how, when, and where we get our news.

items.title: Your brain was never designed for this much bad news

clusters.title: Social determinants of health can match or beat genetic risk in predicting some common diseases
clusters.summary: A new study from the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai shows that social determinants of health—including environmental conditions, health behaviors, access to resources and social well-being—can play an equally important or even greater role than genetics in predicting a person's risk of developing common diseases.

items.title: Social determinants of health can match or beat genetic risk in predicting some common diseases
items.description: A new study from the Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai shows that social determinants of health—including environmental conditions, health behaviors, access to resources and social well-being—can play an equally important or even greater role than genetics in predicting a person's risk of developing common diseases.

clusters.title: How RSV manipulates the immune response in respiratory cells
clusters.summary: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) can cause severe lower respiratory tract infections, particularly in newborns and older adults. How the virus manages to evade the immune system and what changes it triggers in infected cells have not been fully understood. Researchers from TWINCORE, Center for Experimental and Clinical Infection Research, together with interdisciplinary colleagues from Würzburg, Regensburg, Braunschweig and Hannover, have now demonstrated how the virus interferes with the genetic program of respiratory cells, inhibits the immune response and disrupts cell function.

items.title: How RSV manipulates the immune response in respiratory cells
items.description: Respiratory syncytial virus (RSV) can cause severe lower respiratory tract infections, particularly in newborns and older adults. How the virus manages to evade the immune system and what changes it triggers in infected cells have not been fully understood. Researchers from TWINCORE, Center for Experimental and Clinical Infection Research, together with interdisciplinary colleagues from Würzburg, Regensburg, Braunschweig and Hannover, have now demonstrated how the virus interferes with the genetic program of respiratory cells, inhibits the immune response and disrupts cell function. They published their findings in the journal Science Advances.

clusters.title: Scientists uncover the physical signs of lucid dreaming in people with trauma symptoms
clusters.summary: New research shows that specific sleep patterns, like interrupted rest and falling asleep quickly, can predict lucid dreaming in people with PTSD. This could eventually help therapists use conscious dreaming to treat trauma-related nightmares.

items.title: Scientists uncover the physical signs of lucid dreaming in people with trauma symptoms
items.description: New research shows that specific sleep patterns, like interrupted rest and falling asleep quickly, can predict lucid dreaming in people with PTSD. This could eventually help therapists use conscious dreaming to treat trauma-related nightmares.

clusters.title: Popular anti-aging drug combo caused severe brain damage in mice
clusters.summary: A drug combo widely explored for anti-aging may actually damage the brain, according to new mouse research showing severe loss of myelin and changes linked to “chemo brain.” Surprisingly, the damaged cells resembled those seen in multiple sclerosis, giving scientists a new lead in understanding—and potentially repairing—the disease.

items.title: Popular anti-aging drug combo caused severe brain damage in mice
items.description: A drug combo widely explored for anti-aging may actually damage the brain, according to new mouse research showing severe loss of myelin and changes linked to “chemo brain.” Surprisingly, the damaged cells resembled those seen in multiple sclerosis, giving scientists a new lead in understanding—and potentially repairing—the disease.

clusters.title: Your brain starts making social decisions before you do
clusters.summary: Researchers found that social behavior begins in the brain before it becomes visible as movement. In zebrafish, a coordinated pattern of activity spread across the brain several seconds before the animals approached another fish. A higher brain region called the pallium played a key role, and fish with stronger neural signals were generally more social.

clusters.title: Arrests of Immigrant Parents Create Mental Health Crisis for Children
clusters.summary: An estimated hundreds of thousands of children, many of them U.S. citizens, have been separated from a parent in the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown. Their distress manifests in physical and mental health symptoms including developmental regression, stomachaches, sleep problems, and falling grades.

items.title: Arrests of Immigrant Parents Create Mental Health Crisis for Children
items.description: An estimated hundreds of thousands of children, many of them U.S. citizens, have been separated from a parent in the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown. Their distress manifests in physical and mental health symptoms including developmental regression, stomachaches, sleep problems, and falling grades. Research points to long-term health consequences.
